claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Let me start by gathering some basic information.

Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. I'm inquiring about my son Alex. He's 19 years old and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Since Alex is 19 years old, I need to understand his current situation, as eligibility rules change once children reach 16.

Is Alex currently in approved education or training (such as full-time non-advanced education or an approved training program)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he's in education. He's doing his A-levels at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful to know. A-levels would typically count as approved education.

To confirm his eligibility, I need to check: Did Alex start or is he continuing this A-level course before his 20th birthday?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he did. He's been doing the course and he's not 20 yet, so he started before turning 20.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. Now I need to understand your relationship to Alex and your living situation.

What is your relationship to Alex - are you his parent or guardian? And do you have responsibility for Alex (meaning you're responsible for his care and upbringing)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm his parent and I have responsibility for him. He lives here with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just a few more questions to complete the assessment.

Are you currently living in the UK, and does Alex also live in the UK with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're in Manchester. And yes, Alex lives here with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Just one final question to complete the assessment:

Is Alex currently in hospital care or local authority care (such as in foster care or a children's home)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex has actually been in hospital for a while now - about 11 weeks. But I still help out and stay involved in his care while he's there.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for sharing that. This is an important detail. Let me read the specific guidance about children in hospital to give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. I now have the information I need. I have one more important question based on the rules:

You mentioned Alex has been in hospital for about 11 weeks. Do you regularly spend money on Alex while he's in hospital (for example, on things like clothes, food, toiletries, or other items for him)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I still buy things for him - you know, clothes, magazines, his favorite snacks and things like that. I try to keep him comfortable while he's in there.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have everything I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:

✓ **Age and education**: Alex is 19 and studying A-levels (approved education) which he started before turning 20 - this meets the requirements for children aged 16-20.

✓ **Responsibility**: You're his parent and have responsibility for him.

✓ **UK residence**: You both live in the UK.

✓ **Hospital stay**: Although Alex has been in hospital for about 11 weeks, you continue to regularly spend money on him (clothes, magazines, snacks, etc.). This is important because there's an exception to the hospital stay rules - you can still receive Child Benefit for a child in a UK hospital if you regularly spend money on them, even after the usual 12-week limit.

You should inform HMRC about Alex being in hospital, as they ask you to notify them when a child is in hospital for more than 12 weeks, but based on your circumstances, your Child Benefit should continue.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 19 years old and in approved education (A-levels), which he started before turning 20, so he qualifies under the rules for children aged 16-20 in approved education or training. You are his parent, have responsibility for him, and both live in the UK (Manchester). Although Alex has been in hospital for approximately 11 weeks (which is under the 12-week threshold), and even beyond that threshold, you would still qualify for Child Benefit because you regularly spend money on him while he's in hospital (buying clothes, magazines, snacks, etc.). According to the rules, there is an exception to the 12-week limit for children in a UK hospital if you regularly spend money on them. Therefore, you meet all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. Alex meets the age and education requirements (19 years old, in A-levels started before age 20), the claimant is his parent with responsibility for him, they both live in the UK, and although Alex has been in hospital for 11 weeks, the claimant regularly spends money on him, which means they qualify under the exception to the hospital stay time limit.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 19 years old and in approved education (A-levels), which he started before turning 20, so he qualifies under the rules for children aged 16-20 in approved education or training. You are his parent, have responsibility for him, and both live in the UK (Manchester). Although Alex has been in hospital for approximately 11 weeks (which is under the 12-week threshold), and even beyond that threshold, you would still qualify for Child Benefit because you regularly spend money on him while he's in hospital (buying clothes, magazines, snacks, etc.). According to the rules, there is an exception to the 12-week limit for children in a UK hospital if you regularly spend money on them. Therefore, you meet all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. Alex meets the age and education requirements (19 years old, in A-levels started before age 20), the claimant is his parent with responsibility for him, they both live in the UK, and although Alex has been in hospital for 11 weeks, the claimant regularly spends money on him, which means they qualify under the exception to the hospital stay time limit.
